summary: DHH recounts his frustrating experience as a longtime Sonos megafan whose multi-room music system became unreliable, pushing him to explore competitors like Bluesound and WiiM. After months of debugging hardwired Sonos equipment with no resolution, he discovered that cheaper alternatives worked instantly and that Sonos's historic app advantage had been eroded by Spotify Connect. He draws a broader business lesson: once you force satisfied customers back into the market to sample competitors, you risk losing them permanently. He cites Basecamp's approach of maintaining legacy versions for happy customers as the right model, arguing that the cost of keeping existing customers content is far less than the cost of losing them.
key_points: Sonos's reliability problems—not just the app but firmware and connectivity—destroyed the 'it just works' experience that justified customer loyalty
Spotify Connect and cheaper competitors like WiiM ($299 vs $599) have eliminated Sonos's historic moat of app superiority and multi-room grouping
The worst thing a business can do is push satisfied customers back into the market where they discover viable alternatives
37signals runs three versions of Basecamp, including one unchanged for 14 years, specifically to avoid disrupting happy customers
The cost of maintaining backward compatibility and legacy products is worth paying compared to the cost of customer defection
money_quotes: When you want to listen to some music at dinner, you want play to mean PLAY RIGHT NOW. Not "won't connect". Not "try restarting the app". Just play.
If you've managed to win over a customer, the last thing you want to do is get them back into the market to sample the competition.
If you can keep them content with what they got, they aren't interested in whatever anyone else is offering.
That's why it's worth going the extra effort not to fuck with existing customers.
I'd been boiled by the Sonos problems for so long that I didn't even remember what instant does for enjoyment. It does a lot!
Don't push customers back into the market. They might just find that Spotify Connect is pretty awesome, and that amps half the price can do the job.
They signed up for a system they liked in the mid-to-late 2000s, and they're not in the market for a change. THAT'S JUST FINE BY US!
key_insight: The greatest competitive advantage is customer inertia—never force satisfied customers to re-evaluate their options, because the market they return to may have changed dramatically since they last looked.
